content:
# Genesis Chapter 21 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11v–12r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_021_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_021_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 16–17 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**Manuscript Confirmation:** The Leningrad Codex images (Folios 11v–12r, West Semitic Research Project, © Bruce E. Zuckerman) have been read directly by all three council translators. Folio 11v (GEN_021_page_001.jpg) opens with the birth fulfillment notice (v. 1) immediately following the close of Genesis 20, and carries the narrative through the wilderness episode to approximately verse 21, in the characteristic three-column Tiberian Masoretic layout with full niqqud and te'amim. Folio 12r (GEN_021_page_002.jpg) carries the Abimelech covenant episode (vv. 22–34), concluding the chapter before Genesis 22 begins in the same column. The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 16–17) has been read directly; the chapter marker "CAP. XXIII" (in the older Greek enumeration) is visible in the top margin of page 16. No Dead Sea Scrolls fragments of Genesis 21 are extant; the Leningrad Codex is the sole primary Hebrew witness for this chapter.

---

*The Birth of Isaac — The LORD Visits Sarah (vv. 1–7)*

**21:1** The LORD visited Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did to Sarah as he had promised.

**21:2** And Sarah conceived and bore Abraham a son in his old age at the appointed time of which God had spoken to him.

**21:3** Abraham called the name of his son who was born to him — whom Sarah bore to him — Isaac.

**21:4** And Abraham circumcised his son Isaac when he was eight days old, as God had commanded him.

**21:5** Abraham was a hundred years old when his son Isaac was born to him.

**21:6** And Sarah said, "God has made laughter for me — everyone who hears will laugh with me."

**21:7** And she said, "Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children? Yet I have borne him a son in his old age."

---

*The Weaning Feast — Sarah Demands the Expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael (vv. 8–13)*

**21:8** And the child grew and was weaned. And Abraham made a great feast on the day that Isaac was weaned.

**21:9** But Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian — whom she had borne to Abraham — laughing. ⚠️

**21:10** So she said to Abraham, "Cast out this slave woman and her son, for the son of this slave woman shall not be heir with my son Isaac."

**21:11** And the thing was very displeasing in the eyes of Abraham on account of his son.

**21:12** But God said to Abraham, "Do not be displeased on account of the boy and on account of your slave woman. Whatever Sarah says to you, listen to her voice, for through Isaac shall your offspring be named. ⚠️

**21:13** And I will also make a nation of the son of the slave woman, because he is your offspring."

---

*Hagar and Ishmael in the Wilderness of Beer-sheba (vv. 14–21)*

**21:14** So Abraham rose early in the morning and took bread and a skin of water and gave them to Hagar, placing them on her shoulder, along with the child, and sent her away. She departed and wandered in the wilderness of Beer-sheba.

**21:15** When the water in the skin was gone, she cast the child under one of the bushes.

**21:16** Then she went and sat down opposite him at a distance — about a bowshot away — for she said, "Let me not look on the death of the child." And as she sat opposite him, she lifted up her voice and wept.

**21:17** And God heard the voice of the boy, and the angel of God called to Hagar from heaven and said to her, "What is the matter, Hagar? Do not be afraid, for God has heard the voice of the boy where he is.

**21:18** Rise up, lift up the boy and hold him fast with your hand, for I will make him into a great nation."

**21:19** Then God opened her eyes and she saw a well of water. She went and filled the skin with water and gave the boy a drink.

**21:20** And God was with the boy as he grew up. He lived in the wilderness and became an archer.

**21:21** He lived in the wilderness of Paran, and his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt.

---

*Abimelech's Covenant with Abraham at Beer-sheba (vv. 22–34)*

**21:22** At that time Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his army said to Abraham, "God is with you in all that you do.

**21:23** Now therefore swear to me here by God that you will not deal falsely with me or with my offspring or with my descendants. According to the loyalty I have shown you, you shall show to me and to the land in which you have sojourned."

**21:24** And Abraham said, "I will swear."

**21:25** But Abraham reproved Abimelech on account of a well of water that Abimelech's servants had seized.

**21:26** And Abimelech said, "I do not know who did this thing. You did not tell me, and I have not heard of it until today."

**21:27** So Abraham took sheep and cattle and gave them to Abimelech, and the two men made a covenant.

**21:28** Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs of the flock by themselves.

**21:29** And Abimelech said to Abraham, "What are these seven ewe lambs that you have set apart by themselves?"

**21:30** He said, "The seven ewe lambs you shall take from my hand, so that this may be a witness for me that I dug this well."

**21:31** Therefore that place was called Beer-sheba, because there the two of them swore an oath. ⚠️

**21:32** So they made a covenant at Beer-sheba. Then Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his army rose up and returned to the land of the Philistines.

**21:33** Abraham planted a tamarisk tree at Beer-sheba and called there on the name of the LORD, the Everlasting God. ⚠️

**21:34** And Abraham sojourned many days in the land of the Philistines.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### On the Architecture of Genesis 21 — Four Movements

All three council translators identified the same four narrative movements in this chapter, and the council adopts their shared structural analysis:

1. **vv. 1–7** — The birth and naming of Isaac: fulfillment of the promise.
2. **vv. 8–21** — The weaning feast, the expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael, and their rescue in the wilderness.
3. **vv. 22–32** — Abimelech's covenant with Abraham at Beer-sheba.
4. **vv. 33–34** — The planting of the tamarisk and the invocation of YHWH El Olam.

PNEUMA's summary observation is adopted as the council's governing frame: the wordplay on *tsechoq* / *yitschaq* (צְחֹק / יִצְחָק — laughter / Isaac) is not ornamental but structural — the spine of the chapter. Sarah laughed in disbelief (18:12); now God has made laughter for her (21:6); then Ishmael does something with that same root (21:9, *metsacheq*), and the doing of it sets in motion the expulsion. The boy of laughter (*Yitschaq*) remains in the covenant line; the boy who laughed (*metsacheq*) departs — but he too is heard. EMET's note on the four specific occurrences of the root in the chapter (vv. 3, 6 [twice], 9) is adopted in full.

---

### On 21:1 — "The LORD visited Sarah" — פָּקַד (*paqad*)

All three translators agreed on "visited" as the rendering of *pāqad* (פָּקַד), and the council follows unanimously (3–0). EMET and LOGOS both documented the full semantic range of *pāqad* (to visit, attend to, muster, appoint, remember with purposeful action) and both noted that the LXX Vaticanus renders it *epeskepsato* (ἐπεσκέψατο), the standard Greek equivalent, corresponding exactly. PNEUMA dynamicized to "kept his word," which — while capturing the *result* of the divine visitation — loses the active, personal, visiting character of the Hebrew verb and the specific LXX correspondence. The council retains "visited" as the most precise and widely supported rendering. LOGOS's note on the verbal connection to Luke 1:68, 78 (*episkeptomai* in the Benedictus) is adopted as theologically significant.

The doubled fulfillment formula in v. 1 — "as he had said... as he had promised" — uses two different verbs for speech (*'āmar*, ordinary speech; *dibber*, the weightier declarative verb). EMET and LOGOS both preserved this distinction; PNEUMA conflated them. The council's rendering: "as he had said" (for *'āmar*) and "as he had promised" (for *dibber* in fulfillment context). The deliberate emphasis of the double clause — the narrator's insistence that every word of the promise was fulfilled — is preserved.

---

### On 21:2 — "at the appointed time" (*lammô'ēd*, לַמּוֹעֵד)

All three translators agreed on "at the appointed time." The council follows unanimously. EMET and LOGOS both noted that *mô'ēd* (מוֹעֵד) carries covenant resonance — an appointment made by God's word in Gen 18:14 ("at the appointed time I will return") is now kept. The LXX renders it *kata ton kairon*, "according to the season/time." This phrase is the temporal marker of covenant faithfulness: the promise arrives exactly when it was scheduled.

---

### On 21:3 — The double relative clause

EMET's note on the doubled relative clause — "the one born to him... whom Sarah bore to him" — is adopted by the council. The grammatical accumulation insists simultaneously on both paternity (born to Abraham) and maternity (Sarah bore him), establishing the dual genealogical legitimacy of Isaac as the heir of both parents. LOGOS and PNEUMA both understood this but simplified it differently. The council adopts the rendering that preserves the double clause without the full Hebraistic awkwardness of EMET's version: "his son who was born to him — whom Sarah bore to him — Isaac."

---

### On 21:5 — Age notice

EMET rendered the Hebrew idiom *ben-me'at shanah* ("son of a hundred years") literally; LOGOS and PNEUMA both smoothed it to "a hundred years old." The council follows LOGOS and PNEUMA — "Abraham was a hundred years old" — since the literal "son of a hundred years" would be genuinely opaque to English readers and the birth-record notice is the communicative intent, not the grammatical form.

---

### On 21:6 — "God has made laughter for me" — the tsechoq wordplay and *lî*

See Disagreement Log below for the full adjudication of *lî* ("with me" vs. "at me"). The council rules for "laugh with me."

The fronted position of *tsechoq* (צְחֹק) at the head of Sarah's speech is preserved in the council's rendering: "God has made laughter for me." EMET's fully fronted literal version ("Laughter — God has made for me") preserves the Hebrew word order but is awkward in English; the council adopts the LOGOS version, which achieves the same semantic foreground — *laughter* is what God has made — in natural English.

PNEUMA's rendering "God has given me something to laugh about" captures the idiomatic sense but disperses the concentrated force of *tsechoq* as a single word standing alone. The council prefers "God has made laughter for me" as the tightest English equivalent that preserves the noun *laughter* (which resonates with the name Isaac) at the center of Sarah's exclamation.

---

### On 21:7 — "Who would have said" (*mî millēl*)

All three translators agreed on "Who would have said to Abraham." EMET noted the lexical rarity of *millēl* (a Piel of an archaic root, found only here in the Pentateuch in this form). Sarah's speech in the third person about herself — "Sarah would nurse children" — is preserved in the council's rendering, as EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all retained it. The rhetorical distancing (speaking of herself as "Sarah") underscores the continued inconceivability of the event even as she celebrates it.

---

### On 21:8 — The weaning feast

All three translators agreed in substance. The council adopts LOGOS's formulation — "on the day that Isaac was weaned" — over EMET's more literal "on the day of the weaning of Isaac." The phrase is equivalent in meaning; the LOGOS version is more natural.

---

### On 21:9 — *metsacheq* — what was Ishmael doing? ⚠️

See Disagreement Log below for the full discussion. The council rules for "laughing" following all three translators, with the full crux documented.

---

### On 21:10 — "Cast out" (*gārēsh*, גָּרֵשׁ) and "slave woman" (*'āmāh*)

All three translators used "cast out" (or "throw out" — PNEUMA) for *gārash*. The council follows EMET and LOGOS: "Cast out." EMET and LOGOS both noted the force of this verb — the same word used for the expulsion from Eden (Gen 3:24) and the driving out of nations from the land. PNEUMA's "throw out" is slightly colloquial; the council prefers "cast out" for its formal, legal force.

EMET's analysis of *'āmāh* (אָמָה, female slave) as Sarah's deliberately dehumanizing register — "this slave woman," never Hagar's name — is adopted by the council. The term is preserved throughout. The council notes that God also uses *'āmāh* at vv. 12–13, picking up Sarah's register, and that the distinction between *'āmāh* and *šiphchāh* (the term used for Hagar in ch. 16) is noted by EMET but does not alter the consensus rendering.

---

### On 21:11 — "Very displeasing in the eyes of Abraham"

EMET rendered "very grievous in the eyes of Abraham"; LOGOS rendered "very displeasing to Abraham"; PNEUMA rendered "deeply distressed Abraham." The Hebrew *wayyēra' haddāvār me'ōd be'ênê 'Avrāhām* (וַיֵּרַע הַדָּבָר מְאֹד בְּעֵינֵי אַבְרָהָם) is literally "the thing was very evil/bad in the eyes of Abraham." PNEUMA noted that the word *ra'* (רַע) carries moral weight, not just emotional distress — Abraham regarded what was being asked of him as *wrong*. The council adopts the rendering "very displeasing in the eyes of Abraham" — closer to the literal idiom than PNEUMA's paraphrase, and slightly more emotionally precise than EMET's "grievous." The "in the eyes of" construction is retained as a Hebrew idiom that natural English can carry.

---

### On 21:12 — "Through Isaac shall your offspring be named" — the key promise ⚠️

See Disagreement Log below. The council rules for "through Isaac shall your offspring be named" — following EMET and LOGOS.

The divine instruction "listen to her voice" (*šema' beqōlāh*, שְׁמַע בְּקֹלָהּ) — directed to Abraham regarding Sarah's demand — is rendered uniformly by all three translators. EMET rendered "hear her voice"; LOGOS rendered "listen to her voice." The council follows LOGOS: "listen to her voice" is more naturally imperatival in English.

---

### On 21:13 — "Also the son of the slave woman" — the *gam* particle

All three translators preserved the *gam* (גַּם, "also / even") at the head of God's promise about Ishmael, and the council follows. PNEUMA's note is adopted: the *gam* is significant — God is not dismissing Ishmael but redirecting him. Both sons receive divine provision; only one carries the covenant line. "And I will also make a nation of the son of the slave woman" captures the emphasis.

---

### On 21:14 — Hagar's departure — the shoulder / child syntactic ambiguity

All three translators acknowledged the syntactic difficulty of this verse but rendered it similarly: Abraham gave Hagar the provisions, placed them on her shoulder, and sent her away with the child. The council adopts this reading as the most natural rendering of the Hebrew, noting the ambiguity in the council's notes rather than resolving it by textual emendation.

EMET preserved "wandered" (*wattēta'*, וַתֵּתַע) for the verb of Hagar's movement in the wilderness; LOGOS and PNEUMA also used "wandered." The council follows unanimously — the verb carries the sense of directionless, desperate wandering, not purposeful travel.

---

### On 21:15 — "Cast the child" (*šālak*, שָׁלַךְ)

EMET rendered "cast the child"; LOGOS rendered "put the child"; PNEUMA rendered "pushed the boy." The Hebrew *watašlēk* (וַתַּשְׁלֵךְ) is the Hiphil of *šālak*, meaning to throw, cast, hurl — a strong verb. The LXX Vaticanus renders *erripsen* (ἔρριψεν), equally strong ("hurled"). The council rules for "cast" — following EMET and the LXX — over LOGOS's softened "put." EMET's note is adopted: this is not a gentle laying-down; it conveys the extremity of Hagar's despair. The child is dying; she cannot carry him further; she puts him under the only shade available and removes herself.

---

### On 21:16 — "About a bowshot away"

All three translators preserved the rare expression *kimitachăwê qešet* (כִּמְטַחֲוֵי קֶשֶׁת), "as it were a bowshot." EMET and LOGOS rendered "about a bowshot"; PNEUMA rendered "about a bowshot away." The council adopts "about a bowshot away" as the most complete equivalent. EMET's note on the rarity of the expression (a hapax legomenon) and the precision it conveys — close enough to hear, far enough not to watch — is adopted in full. The LXX renders it *hōsei toxou bolēn* ("as if a bowshot"), confirming the sense.

---

### On 21:17 — "What is the matter, Hagar?"

EMET rendered "What is wrong with you"; LOGOS rendered "What troubles you"; PNEUMA rendered "What is the matter." The Hebrew *mah-lāk* (מַה-לָּךְ) is literally "What is to you?" — an idiom for "What is the matter with you?" All three capture the sense; the council adopts PNEUMA's "What is the matter, Hagar?" as the most natural English equivalent that preserves the address to Hagar by name. EMET and LOGOS noted the verbal parallel with Gen 16:8, where the same phrase opens the angel's address to Hagar at the spring. The recurrence is deliberate — the same woman, twice in the wilderness, twice encountered by heaven — and the council notes it here.

The council also adopts EMET's theological note: the name *Yishmā'ēl* (יִשְׁמָעֵאל, "God hears") is enacted in this verse — "God heard the voice of the boy." The promise embedded in the name given before his birth (16:11) is fulfilled at the lowest point of his life.

---

### On 21:18 — "Hold him fast with your hand"

EMET rendered "hold him by your hand"; LOGOS rendered "hold him fast with your hand"; PNEUMA rendered "hold him close." The Hebrew *wehachaziqî 'et-yādēk bô* (וְהַחֲזִיקִי אֶת-יָדֵךְ בּוֹ) literally means "strengthen your hand in/on him" — a firm grip, not an embrace. The council follows LOGOS: "hold him fast with your hand." PNEUMA's "hold him close" is emotionally apt but substitutes intimacy for the Hebrew's physical firmness (the verb *chāzaq* means to be strong, to grasp firmly).

---

### On 21:19 — "God opened her eyes"

All three translators agreed on "God opened her eyes." The council follows unanimously. EMET's note is adopted: the verb *pāqach* (פָּקַח, "to open eyes") signals that the well was already there — the miracle is not creation of water but opening of perception. The parallel with 2 Kgs 6:17 (Elisha's servant's eyes opened to see the heavenly army) and Gen 3:7 (Adam and Eve's eyes opened) is noted.

---

### On 21:20 — "Became an archer"

EMET rendered "a shooter of the bow"; LOGOS rendered "an expert with the bow"; PNEUMA rendered "a skilled archer." The Hebrew *rōbeh qaššāt* (רֹבֶה קַשָּׁת) is literally "one who shoots/draws the bow." The council rules for "an archer" as the natural English term — PNEUMA's "skilled" and LOGOS's "expert" both add a qualifier not in the Hebrew. The LXX renders *toxotēs* ("archer"), a clean equivalent without the added modifier.

---

### On 21:21 — The wilderness of Paran

All three translators agreed. "He lived in the wilderness of Paran, and his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt." The council follows unanimously. PNEUMA's note — that Ishmael's Egyptian mother obtaining an Egyptian wife for him represents a return to her homeland in the next generation — is adopted as a narrative observation.

---

### On 21:22 — "At that time" — the Abimelech scene transition

All three translators agreed on "at that time." The council follows. PNEUMA's observation is adopted: Abimelech's testimony — "God is with you in everything you do" — is a remarkable external validation of divine presence, spoken by a Philistine king who has no stake in the covenant narrative.

---

### On 21:23 — "My offspring and my descendants" (*nînî ûnekdî*)

EMET rendered "my offspring and my descendants" — preserving the rare paired terms *nîn* (נִין) and *neked* (נֶכֶד) as a generational hendiadys. LOGOS rendered "my children or with my descendants." PNEUMA rendered "my children, or with my descendants." The council follows EMET's paired rendering — "my offspring or with my descendants" — which preserves the generational scope of Abimelech's request without conflating the two distinct terms.

The rendering of *chesed* (חֶסֶד) in this verse — "According to the loyalty I have shown you, you shall show to me" — follows the council's established convention from Genesis 20 (where SOPHIA ruled 2–1 for "loyalty" over "kindness"). The term in this context is a committed, binding relational obligation, not a spontaneous act of generosity.

---

### On 21:25 — "Reproved" (*hôkîach*)

All three translators used "reproved" (EMET, LOGOS) or "confronted" (PNEUMA). The council follows EMET and LOGOS: "reproved." The Hiphil of *yākach* (יָכַח) carries the formal sense of a legal or interpersonal protest/complaint — Abraham formally registers his grievance. "Confronted" (PNEUMA) captures the personal dynamism but loses the formal-legal register of the verb.

---

### On 21:27 — "Sheep and cattle"

LOGOS rendered "sheep and oxen"; EMET and PNEUMA rendered "sheep and cattle." The council follows EMET and PNEUMA — "cattle" is the broader and more accurate rendering of *bāqār* (בָּקָר) than "oxen," which specifies working bovines.

---

### On 21:28–30 — The seven ewe lambs

All three translators agreed in substance. The council follows LOGOS's formulation for v. 30: "so that this may be a witness for me that I dug this well." EMET's rendering "in order that it may be for me a witness" is more literal but slightly awkward; PNEUMA's "They will serve as proof" loses the formal-legal term "witness" (*'ēdāh*, עֵדָה) — a specific covenantal and legal term for testimony, not merely proof.

---

### On 21:31 — Beer-sheba: the double etymology ⚠️

See Disagreement Log below.

---

### On 21:33 — The tamarisk tree and YHWH El Olam ⚠️

See Disagreement Log below for the *'ēshel* question. The divine title *'Ēl 'Ôlām* (אֵל עוֹלָם) — "the Everlasting God" — is unanimous across all three translators. The council follows. EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA each independently noted the unique occurrence of this divine epithet in Genesis, the long-duration meaning of *'ôlām* (עוֹלָם), and the significance of Abraham invoking the Eternal God at a covenantal site at the end of a chapter dominated by questions of futures and offspring. PNEUMA's observation is adopted: to plant a long-lived tree and call on the Eternal God at a place named for an oath is a gesture of permanence — these covenants, these children, these wells belong to the God who outlasts all of them.

---

### On 21:34 — "Sojourned" (*wayyāgār*)

All three translators agreed on "sojourned." The council follows unanimously. EMET's note is adopted: the verb *gûr* (גּוּר, "to sojourn as a resident alien") used here closes the chapter with the same term that described Abraham's status in Gen 20:1. Even after covenant-making and the naming of holy sites, Abraham remains a landless sojourner in the land. He participates in its covenants but never owns it — a theological datum as much as a biographical notice.

---

### The Moral and Theological Architecture of Genesis 21

The council adopts PNEUMA's summary statement as the governing theological frame for this chapter: Genesis 21 is a chapter about division and provision. The covenant family divides — Isaac is kept in the covenant line, Ishmael is sent away — but both sons receive divine provision. Isaac receives it through the covenant promise; Ishmael receives it through God's direct hearing of a dying boy's cry in the desert. God is not absent from the expelled story. God is present in both branches.

LOGOS's structural parallel between Genesis 16 (the first Hagar narrative) and Genesis 21:8–21 is adopted as a key interpretive framework:

| Genesis 16 | Genesis 21 |
|---|---|
| Hagar flees (v. 6) | Hagar is sent away (v. 14) |
| Hagar in the wilderness (v. 7) | Hagar in the wilderness of Beer-sheba (v. 14) |
| Angel of the LORD finds her by a spring (v. 7) | God hears the boy; angel calls from heaven (v. 17) |
| "Return and submit" (v. 9) | "Rise up, lift up the boy" (v. 18) |
| "I will multiply your offspring" (v. 10) | "I will make him a great nation" (v. 18) |
| Name prophecy: Ishmael (v. 11) | Name enacted: "God heard the voice of the boy" (v. 17) |
| "You are a God of seeing" — El Roi (v. 13) | "God opened her eyes" (v. 19) |

The *tsechoq* wordplay, the double naming of Beer-sheba, and the invocation of *El Olam* at the chapter's close together form a literary architecture of extraordinary density: the boy of laughter establishes the site of the oath, and both are placed under the care of the God who endures beyond all of it.

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### 21:9 — *metsacheq* — "laughing," "playing," or "mocking"?

**The Question:** What was Ishmael doing when Sarah saw him? The Hebrew participle *metsacheq* (מְצַחֵק) — the Piel of the root *ts-ch-q*, the same root as Isaac's name — is deliberately ambiguous. What word should translate it, and should the LXX's addition ("with Isaac her son") be acknowledged in the text?

**EMET's Position:** Rendered "laughing" — the most literal, root-level rendering of *ts-ch-q*. EMET documented four possible ranges of the Piel: (a) laughing/rejoicing; (b) playing (as a child); (c) mocking/scorning; (d) sexual activity (as in Gen 26:8; 39:14, 17). EMET retained "laughing" to hold all four possibilities open, noting that the LXX Vaticanus adds *meta Isaak tou huiou autēs* ("with Isaac her son") — a phrase absent from the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex — as a clarifying gloss that shapes the scene toward innocent play.

**LOGOS's Position:** Also rendered "laughing." Documented the same fourfold range as EMET, provided a detailed comparison table of the MT and LXX, and confirmed that the LXX addition is not in the MT. Noted Paul's reading in Galatians 4:29 ("he who was born according to the flesh persecuted him who was born according to the Spirit") as dependent on a tradition of *metsacheq* as mockery or persecution — perhaps the LXX tradition that specified a target (Isaac).

**PNEUMA's Position:** Also rendered "laughing." Provided the most nuanced narrative analysis: the ambiguity is almost certainly intentional. The narrator gives only the bare fact — Ishmael was *metsacheq* — and lets Sarah's reaction carry the weight. PNEUMA observed that the reader stands where Sarah stood: watching a scene that could be read multiple ways, trying to understand why it troubled her so deeply.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **3–0 for "laughing"** — unanimous. All three translators independently arrived at the same word, all three documented the same interpretive range, and all three preserved the ambiguity rather than resolving it.

The council declines to incorporate the LXX addition ("with Isaac her son") into the translation text, following the Masoretic Hebrew of the Leningrad Codex. The LXX addition is documented here as a significant textual variant that shaped the patristic and Pauline interpretive tradition. It was almost certainly inserted to clarify the scene as innocent play (making Ishmael's target explicit) and thereby remove the ambiguity the Hebrew sustains deliberately.

The ambiguity of *metsacheq* stands as an intentional literary feature: the root of Isaac's own name (*Yitschaq*) is used to describe whatever Ishmael was doing. Whether he was playing, laughing in joy, mocking, or something more threatening — the text uses the one word that most implicates the name and identity of the child of promise. It is an act of verbal precision so refined it cannot be rendered without remainder in any translation. The council's rendering "laughing" holds the ambiguity open. The reader must decide — or, more accurately, the text does not permit the reader to decide with certainty.

Paul's reading (Gal 4:29) represents one strand of the interpretive tradition; the rabbinic discussions of this verse represent others. The Hebrew text, as it stands in the Leningrad Codex, bears all of them.

---

### 21:12 — "Through Isaac shall your offspring be named" — the force of *bĕyitschāq*

**The Question:** The preposition *bĕ* (בְּ) before Isaac's name in *kî bĕyitschāq yiqqārē' lĕkā zāra'* can mean "in Isaac" (locative — within Isaac's line) or "through Isaac" (instrumental — by means of Isaac). Which English preposition best captures the meaning, and how should the passive *yiqqārē'* ("will be called/named") be rendered?

**EMET's Position:** Rendered "through Isaac shall offspring be called to you" — using "through" for the instrumental sense of *bĕ* and preserving the Niphal passive *yiqqārē'* as "called."

**LOGOS's Position:** Rendered "through Isaac shall your offspring be named" — using "through" for *bĕ* and "named" for *yiqqārē'*, and providing extensive documentation of Paul's citation of this verse in Romans 9:7 and Galatians 4:30 (via the LXX *en Isaak klēthēsetai soi sperma*, "in Isaac shall your seed be called"). LOGOS argued for "through" to convey the instrumental force — the line of promise is funneled specifically through Isaac.

**PNEUMA's Position:** Rendered "it is through Isaac that your family line will carry your name" — a dynamic paraphrase that captures the meaning (the covenant line runs through Isaac) but loses the passive *yiqqārē'* and adds "family line" and "carry" without Hebrew basis.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **2–1 for "through Isaac shall your offspring be named"** — following EMET and LOGOS over PNEUMA. The reasons:

1. The Niphal passive *yiqqārē'* (יִקָּרֵא) is a theologically significant verb form — the offspring "will be called / named" through Isaac. This is not merely a statement about genealogical descent but about divine naming and election. The passive form matters: it is God who does the naming through Isaac, not merely the human line that runs through him. "Named" preserves this passive force better than PNEUMA's active "carry."

2. "Through" for *bĕ* is supported by both EMET and LOGOS and captures the instrumental sense that Paul himself reads in the LXX version (*en* + dative, Romans 9:7). The promise does not merely locate itself in Isaac's line; it is constituted by passing through him specifically.

3. PNEUMA's "family line" and "carry your name" have no lexical basis in the Hebrew and introduce a metaphor (carrying a name) that the text does not use.

The council notes the extraordinary theological weight of this verse. Paul cites it in Romans 9:7 as the key text for his theology of election: "it is not the children of the flesh who are the children of God, but the children of the promise are counted as offspring." The promise does not run mechanically through all of Abraham's biological descendants — it runs specifically through Isaac, then through Jacob over Esau, then through the line that culminates in the covenant community Paul describes. The verb "shall be named" (*yiqqārē'*) points to divine designation, not natural generation. The council's rendering: **"through Isaac shall your offspring be named."**

---

### 21:31 — Beer-sheba: "well of the oath" or "well of seven"?

**The Question:** The place name *Bĕ'ēr Šāba'* (בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע) sustains a double etymology: (1) "well of the oath" — *bĕ'ēr* (well) + *šāba'* from the root *š-b-'* (to swear); and (2) "well of seven" — *bĕ'ēr* (well) + *šeba'* (seven), pointing to the seven ewe lambs of vv. 28–30. The narrator provides only the "oath" etymology explicitly in v. 31 ("because there both of them swore an oath"), while the "seven" etymology operates structurally through the lambs. How should this be handled in translation?

**EMET's Position:** Retained "Beer-sheba" as the place name and explained both etymologies fully in the notes: the name simultaneously means "well of seven" (the seven ewe lambs) and "well of the oath" (the covenant sworn there). EMET noted that the LXX Vaticanus chose only the "oath" etymology (*Phrear tou horkou*).

**LOGOS's Position:** Also retained "Beer-sheba" as the place name and provided a detailed analysis of the double etymology — noting that both meanings are structurally active in the narrative (seven lambs appear; an oath is sworn) and that the narrator achieves this with a single toponym. LOGOS confirmed the LXX's choice of "well of the oath" as a reduction of the Hebrew's double meaning.

**PNEUMA's Position:** Also retained "Beer-sheba" — explicitly noting that translating the name would collapse the wordplay. PNEUMA explained both etymologies and noted that the "therefore" connecting v. 31 to what precedes it links backward to both the lambs and the oath.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **3–0 to retain "Beer-sheba"** as the transliterated place name — unanimous. All three translators independently reached the same conclusion: translating the name into English would necessarily choose one etymology over the other, destroying the double meaning that is the entire literary point of the scene. The narrator does not choose; both meanings are operative and both are embedded in the narrative fabric. The council preserves "Beer-sheba" and documents both etymologies here.

The council notes that the double meaning is not an accident of etymology but a deliberate literary achievement: *šeba'* (שֶׁבַע, "seven") and *šāba'* (שָׁבַע, "to swear") share the same consonantal root *š-b-'* in Hebrew — the oath was commonly sealed by invoking or offering seven (seven is the number of fullness and completion), and the seven ewe lambs are themselves a form of sworn testimony. The well is the place where both happened: seven lambs were given as witnesses, and an oath was sworn. The name holds them both.

The LXX's choice of "well of the oath" (*Φρέαρ τοῦ ὅρκου*, *Phrear tou horkou*) is a single-meaning interpretation that loses the "seven" dimension — the council follows the MT in preserving both.

---

### 21:33 — *'ēshel* (אֵשֶׁל): "tamarisk tree" or "grove"?

**The Question:** The term *'ēshel* (אֵשֶׁל) is variously rendered as "tamarisk tree" (modern botanical identification), "tree," or "grove" (LXX and KJV tradition). Which is correct?

**EMET's Position:** Rendered "a tamarisk tree" — identifying *'ēshel* botanically as *Tamarix* species, a long-lived, deep-rooted desert tree appropriate for marking a covenantal site. Noted that the LXX Vaticanus renders *'ēshel* as *aroura* (ἄρουρα, "field" or "cultivated land") — a divergence from the arboreal meaning of the Hebrew.

**LOGOS's Position:** Also rendered "a tamarisk tree" — with the same botanical identification and the same note on the LXX's divergent rendering. Noted that the KJV's "grove" misread the term.

**PNEUMA's Position:** Also rendered "a tamarisk tree" — and added the observation that the tamarisk is drought-resistant and long-lived, making it a fitting symbol for covenantal permanence: Abraham plants what will outlast him, at a place named for an oath, and calls on the God who outlasts everything.

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** The council rules **3–0 for "a tamarisk tree"** — unanimous. All three translators independently identified *'ēshel* as the tamarisk and chose the same English rendering. The council adopts PNEUMA's observation on the symbolism of the tamarisk as the fullest theological note on the planting.

The LXX Vaticanus's rendering of *'ēshel* as *aroura* ("field/cultivated land") is a notable divergence — it either reflects a different Hebrew text, a misreading of the consonants, or an interpretive choice to read the planting as establishing cultivated ground rather than planting an individual tree. The council follows the MT.

The council notes that the tamarisk planting and the invocation of YHWH El Olam together constitute the theological climax of the chapter: a long-lived tree planted at a covenanted well, the Everlasting God called upon by name. Beer-sheba is established not merely as a legal landmark but as a sacred site, the southernmost anchor of the patriarchal narrative, which will recur at Genesis 22:19; 26:23–33; 28:10; and 46:1.

---

*— SOPHIA, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
